elements.section-2411-001: Introduction and review of CHY formulas
elements.sent-2411-001: A standard quantum field theoretical treatment of an $n$-gluon scattering process quickly leads to large proliferation of terms.
elements.sent-2411-002: It turns out that, these amplitudes, particularly at tree-level, possess a surprising and beautiful analytical structure, which was first revealed by Parke and Taylor in their seminal calculation of $n$-gluon amplitudes \cite{Parke:1986gb .}
elements.sent-2411-003: Since then, incredible strides have been made to unveil the deep mathematical and analytical structures that underlie scattering amplitudes \cite{Witten:2003nn, Cachazo:2004kj, Britto:2004ap, Roiban:2004yf, Bjerrum-Bohr:2005xoa, Risager:2005vk, britto2005direct, Alex, Bern:2008qj, Arkani-Hamed:2008bsc, Arkani-Hamed:2010zjl, ArkaniHamed:2012nw, Arkani-Hamed:2013jha, Arkani-Hamed:2014bca, Carrasco:2016ygv, Arkani-Hamed:2017mur, Mastrolia:2018uzb, Cachazo:2021wsz , see \cite{ManganoReview, Dixon:1996wi, Cachazo:2005ga, Bern:2007dw, Benincasa:2013faa, Dixon:2013uaa, Elvang:2013cua, Mizera:2019ose, Travaglini:2022uwo, Badger:2023eqz for reviews.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The Cachazo-He-Yuan formulation (CHY) \cite{CHY1, CHY2,CHY3,CHY4,CHY5,Dolan:2013isa of tree-level scattering amplitudes connects the space of Mandelstam invariants $s_{ab =(k_a+k_b)^2$ and the moduli space of punctured Riemann spheres, ${\cal M _{0,n}$, via the scattering equations \cite{CHY2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Scattering amplitudes of massless particles in a large variety of theories can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:genCHY} A^{\rm massless}_{n} = \frac{1}{\mathrm{Vol}(\mathrm{SL}(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\bigg(\sum_{\substack{k=1 k\neq i}}^n \frac{s_{ik}}{u_i- u_k}\bigg)\, \mathcal{I}_L\,\mathcal{I}_R\,,\end{equation} where ${\cal I _L$ and ${\cal I _R$ are functions of the basic $\mathrm{SL (2,\amsbb C )$ covariant factors $1/(u_i-u_j)$, and polynomials in $k_i\cdot k_j$, $k_i\cdot \epsilon_j$ and $\epsilon_i\cdot \epsilon_j$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:genCHY} A^{\rm massless}_{n} = \frac{1}{Vol(SL(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\bigg(\sum_{\substack{k=1 k\neq i}}^n \frac{s_{ik}}{u_i- u_k}\bigg)\, I_L\,I_R\,,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-006: This means that all singularities of the amplitude must be produced as a result of approaching a boundary of the moduli space, ${\cal M _{0,n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-007: The terms in the argument of the Dirac delta are the scattering equations -- there is one equation for each particle $i$.
elements.sent-2411-008: The functions ${\cal I _L$ and ${\cal I _R$ are usually called the ``left" and ``right" integrands.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Each integrand carries the same $\mathrm{SL (2,\amsbb{C )$ weight.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The first tree-level scattering amplitudes that were discovered to have a CHY formulation were those of gluons in pure $U(N)$ Yang-Mills in arbitrary space-time dimensions \cite{CHY2 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The CHY formula for a partial amplitude in the canonical ordering is given by \begin{equation}A^{\rm YM}_{n}(1,2,\ldots ,n) = \frac{1}{\mathrm{Vol}(\mathrm{SL}(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\bigg(\sum_{\substack{k=1 k\neq i}}^n \frac{s_{ik}}{u_i- u_k}\bigg)\mathrm{PT}(1,2,\ldots ,n)\,\Pf' \Psi_{2n}\,,\end{equation} where the two integrands in \eqref{eq:genCHY are \begin{equation}\label{eq:basicIntegrand} {\cal I}_L^{\rm YM}:= \mathrm{PT}(1,2,\ldots ,n):=\frac{1}{(u_1-u_2)(u_2-u_3)\cdots (u_n-u_1)}\quad {\rm and}\quad {\cal I}_R^{\rm YM}:=\Pf' \Psi_{2n}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:basicIntegrand} {\cal I}_L^{\rm YM}:= PT(1,2,\ldots ,n):=\frac{1}{(u_1-u_2)(u_2-u_3)\cdots (u_n-u_1)}\quad {\rm and}\quad {\cal I}_R^{\rm YM}:=\Pf' \Psi_{2n}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-012: The first integrand is known as the Parke-Taylor factor \cite{Parke:1986gb , while the second one is the Pfaffian of $\Psi_{2n}$, a $2n\times 2n$ matrix, given in block form by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Psi_matrix} \Psi_{2n} = \begin{bmatrix} A -C^\intercal C B \end{bmatrix},\end{equation} where $A$ and $B$ are $n\times n$ antisymmetric matrices with off-diagonal components \begin{equation}A_{ab}:= \frac{k_a\cdot k_b}{u_a-u_b} \quad {\rm and} \quad B_{ab}:= \frac{\epsilon_a\cdot \epsilon_b}{u_a-u_b}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The matrix $C$ has components designed to make gauge invariance manifest, \begin{equation}C_{aa}:=-\sum_{\substack{b=1 b\neq a}}^n C_{ab} \quad {\rm and}\quad C_{ab}:= \frac{\epsilon_a\cdot k_b}{u_a-u_b}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}C_{aa}:=-\sum_{\substack{b=1 b\neq a}}^n C_{ab} \quad {\rm and}\quad C_{ab}:= \frac{\epsilon_a\cdot k_b}{u_a-u_b}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-014: Gauge invariance, \ie the vanishing of the amplitude when any polarization vector $\epsilon_a$ is replaced by the momentum $k_a$ of the corresponding particle, manifests simply as a linear algebra property of Pfaffians.
elements.sent-2411-015: Soon after these formulations were discovered, various procedures were introduced to construct new CHY formulations out of known ones \cite{CHY5 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: One such procedure is the compactification of amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-017: Compactifying Yang-Mills amplitudes led to a theory known as Yang-Mills-Scalar (YMS).
elements.sent-2411-018: Higher dimensional gluons with polarization vectors $\epsilon_a$ entirely in the uncompactified dimensions remain as gluons while those $\epsilon_a$ entirely in the internal dimensions are scalars.
elements.sent-2411-019: Particularly simple is the CHY formulation of the pure scalar amplitude in YMS, \begin{equation}\label{eq:YMS} A^{\rm YMS:scalar}_{n}(1,2,\ldots ,n) = \frac{1}{\mathrm{Vol}(\mathrm{SL}(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\!\left(E_i\right)\,\mathrm{PT}(1,2,\ldots ,n)\,\Pf X\,\Pf' A\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: Here $E_i$ is a short-hand notation for the scattering equations, $A$ is the $n\times n$ matrix defined above while $X$ is the matrix obtained from $B$ by setting all $\epsilon_a\cdot \epsilon_b =1$.
elements.sent-2411-021: In recent work \cite{Arkani-Hamed:2023jry , Arkani-Hamed et al. introduced the ``scaffolding'' residue, which is a ``maximal" multi-collinear limit.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Applying the scaffolding residue to an amplitude of $2n$ scalars in the YMS theory leads to an amplitude of $n$ gluons, where polarization vectors emerge from the momenta of the scalars and gauge invariance is geometrized.
elements.sent-2411-023: One of the objectives of this Perimeter Scholars International Winter School Project is the derivation of gluon amplitudes from scalar amplitudes using the scaffolding residue computed using the CHY formulation.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a by-product, the same CHY derivations directly connect $2n$ scalar amplitudes in the Einstein-Maxwell-Scalar (EMS) theory (obtained by compactification of Einstein's theory) to obtain $n$-graviton amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this note we also consider general multi-collinear limits of YMS and extensions to full color-dressed amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-026: This work is organized as follows: in section 2, we introduce the compactification procedure, which allows one to obtain the YMS amplitude~\eqref{eq:YMS .}
elements.sent-2411-027: We proceed to see how a YM amplitude emerges as the most singular term of the YMS amplitude under the scaffolding limit using the CHY formalism.
elements.sent-2411-028: In section 3, we extend the scaffolding analysis to Einstein gravity.
elements.sent-2411-029: Finally, in section 4, we present extensions of the scaffolding residue to possibly non-maximal multi-collinear limits.
elements.sent-2411-030: We show that color-dressed amplitudes also produce the expected singularities under the multi-collinear limits.
elements.section-2411-002: From YM to YMS and back: scaffolding scalar amplitudes in YMS
elements.sent-2411-031: An intricate web of connections among theories of massless particles was constructed using various operations on the CHY formulation of three basic theories \cite{CHY2 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: These are the biadjoint scalar, Yang-Mills, and Einstein Gravity.
elements.sent-2411-033: These are obtained by combining the two basic integrands $\mathrm{PT (1,2,\ldots n)$ and $\Pf'\Psi_{2n}$ presented in \eqref{eq:basicIntegrand .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The simplest operation on CHY integrands is compactification and when applied to the Yang-Mills formula, it leads to the CHY formulation of Yang-Mills-Scalar amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-035: The procedure is to start with an amplitude of $n$ gluons in $D=d+1$ dimensions and set their momenta $\mathcal{K _a$ and polarization vectors $\mathcal{E _a$ to \begin{equation} \label{eq:compactification} K_a := (k_a,0), \quad \forall a \in \{ 1,2,\ldots ,n\} E_a :=  (\epsilon_a,0), for a gluon, (0,0,\ldots, 0,1), for a scalar.  \end{equation} Here $k_a,\epsilon_a$ are $d$-dimensional momenta and polarization vectors respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} \label{eq:compactification} K_a := (k_a,0), \quad \forall a \in \{ 1,2,\ldots ,n\} E_a :=  (\epsilon_a,0), for a gluon, (0,0,\ldots, 0,1), for a scalar.  \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-036: To substitute this into the expression for $\Pf'\Psi_{2n}$ it is convenient to use the indices $p,q,r$ for gluons, $i,j,k$ for scalars and $a,b,c$ when the identity of the particle is not important.
elements.sent-2411-037: Using this, the non-zero inner products in the matrix $\Psi_{2n}$ become \begin{equation}\nonumber \frac{K_a\cdot K_b}{u_a-u_b} = \frac{k_a\cdot k_b}{u_a-u_b}, \qquad \frac{E_p\cdot E_q}{u_p-u_q} = \frac{\epsilon_p\cdot \epsilon_q}{u_p-u_q}, \qquad \frac{E_i\cdot E_j}{u_i-u_j} = \frac{1}{u_i-u_j}\,, \quad \frac{E_p\cdotK_q}{u_p-u_q}=\frac{\epsilon_p\cdot k_q}{u_p-u_q}, \quad and \quad \frac{E_p\cdotK_j}{u_p-u_j}=\frac{\epsilon_p\cdot k_j}{u_p-u_j}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\nonumber \frac{K_a\cdot K_b}{u_a-u_b} = \frac{k_a\cdot k_b}{u_a-u_b}, \qquad \frac{E_p\cdot E_q}{u_p-u_q} = \frac{\epsilon_p\cdot \epsilon_q}{u_p-u_q}, \qquad \frac{E_i\cdot E_j}{u_i-u_j} = \frac{1}{u_i-u_j}\,, \quad \frac{E_p\cdotK_q}{u_p-u_q}=\frac{\epsilon_p\cdot k_q}{u_p-u_q}, \quad and \quad \frac{E_p\cdotK_j}{u_p-u_j}=\frac{\epsilon_p\cdot k_j}{u_p-u_j}\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: Substituting into the $\Psi_{2n}$ matrix with columns and rows organized so that the ``polarization" of scalars are last, one finds \begin{equation}\label{eq:block_compactification} \Psi_{2n} = \begin{bmatrix} A -{\hat C}^\intercal 0 \hat C {\hat B} 0 0 0 X_s \end{bmatrix},\end{equation} where $A$ is the original $n \times n$ matrix $A$ while $\hat B$ only contains gluons.
elements.sent-2411-039: The matrix $X_s$ has components $1/(u_i-u_j)$ and only involves scalars.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our main case of interest is the case when all particles are scalars and therefore $\Pf'\Psi_{2n =\Pf'A\,\, \Pf X_s$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Since there are only scalars in the amplitude we drop the subscript and write $\Pf X_s$ as $\Pf X$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Combining all the pieces we find the CHY formula for the tree-level amplitude of $n$ scalars, \begin{equation}\label{eq:YMS_amplitude} A^{\rm YMS:scalar}_{n}(1,2,\ldots ,n) = \frac{1}{\mathrm{Vol}(\mathrm{SL}(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\!\left(E_i\right)\,\mathrm{PT}(1,2,\ldots ,n)\,\Pf X\,\Pf' A\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: In Ref.~\cite{Arkani-Hamed:2023jry , a new formulation for Yang-Mills scattering amplitudes at any loop order was proposed based on the same combinatorial techniques used for the $\Tr\phi^3$ theory.}
elements.sent-2411-045: One of the key ingredients in making the connection was the use of a multi-collinear limit on Yang-Mills-Scalar amplitudes of $2n$ scalars to produce $n$-gluon amplitudes from ``scalar" data.
elements.sent-2411-046: This procedure was called the ``scaffolding residue", which we diagrammatically represent in figure~\ref{fig:scaffolding .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Given that the CHY formalism straightforwardly connected YM amplitudes to that of YMS scalars, it is natural to see how the scaffolding residue is computed in the CHY formula for scalars \eqref{eq:YMS_amplitude to produce that for gluons.}
elements.sent-2411-048: At first sight, this seems an impossible task since the starting amplitude only contains the Pfaffian of a very simple matrix, the $A$ matrix, while the result should contain the Pfaffian of a matrix with a rich structure, the $\Psi$ matrix.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this section we show in detail how $\Pf'\Psi$ emerges from $\Pf'A$ in a beautiful manner.
elements.sent-2411-050: The scaffolding procedure takes a $2n$-scalar scattering amplitude in the YMS theory and produces an $n$-gluon scattering amplitude in the YM theory under the limit $s_{ii'}\to 0$ for each $1\leq i\leq n$.
elements.sent-2411-051: To this end, let us consider the YMS amplitude with $2n$ particles labelled $\{1,1',\dots, n,n'\}$: \begin{equation} \label{eq:YMS_2n} A^{\rm YMS:scalar}_{2n}(1,1',\ldots ,n, n') = \frac{1}{Vol(SL(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i}\prod_{i'=1'}^{n'}\dd{u_{i'}} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\!\left(E_i\right)\prod_{i'=1'}^{n'}\delta\!\left(E_{i'}\right)\,PT(1,1',\ldots ,n,n')\,\Pf X\,\Pf' A\,. \end{equation} The scaffolding limit $s_{ii'}\to 0$ can be parametrised by a variable $\tau$ and \begin{equation}\label{eq:change_variables}  s_{ii'} =\tau \hat{s}_{ii'}\,, u_{i'} =u_i+\tau x_i\, . \end{equation} Here we have also assumed that $u_{i' -u_i={\cal O (\tau)$.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation} \label{eq:YMS_2n} A^{\rm YMS:scalar}_{2n}(1,1',\ldots ,n, n') = \frac{1}{Vol(SL(2,\amsbb C))}\int\prod_{i=1}^n \dd{u_i}\prod_{i'=1'}^{n'}\dd{u_{i'}} \prod_{i=1}^n\delta\!\left(E_i\right)\prod_{i'=1'}^{n'}\delta\!\left(E_{i'}\right)\,PT(1,1',\ldots ,n,n')\,\Pf X\,\Pf' A\,. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: In general the scattering equations for $2n$ particles have $(2n-3)!$ solutions.
elements.sent-2411-053: However, it is known that in a collinear limit, e.g. $s_{11'}\to 0$, the solutions split into ``singular" and ``regular".
elements.sent-2411-054: The terminology refers to the moduli space of punctured Riemann spheres.
elements.sent-2411-055: Solutions that go to the boundary of the moduli space are called singular while those that remain at generic point are called regular.
elements.sent-2411-056: In a single collinear limit, $s_{11' ={\cal O (\tau)$, singular solutions go as $u_{1' -u_1={\cal O (\tau)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Moreover there are $((2n-1)-3)!$ such singular solutions.
elements.sent-2411-058: Since the scaffolding residue affects compatible channels, we expect that each $s_{ii'}\to 0$ limit can be described as a single collinear limit and hence $u_{i' -u_i={\cal O (\tau)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In the end, after all $n$ collinear limits are implemented, there will only be $(n-3)!$ solutions.
elements.sent-2411-060: Let us introduce the commonly used shorthand notation, \begin{equation}u_{ab} := u_a - u_b.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: We apply the change of variables~\eqref{eq:change_variables to the amplitude in Eq.~\eqref{eq:YMS_2n .}}

elements.sent-2411-179: We make the observation that the multi-collinear limit can be taken sequentially, \ie it is equivalent to first taking $s_{11'}\to 0$, then $s_{22'}\to0$, and so on until $s_{mm'}\to0$.

elements.sent-2411-181: Without loss of generality, it is sufficient to show the single collinear limit $s_{11'}\to 0$ produces the correct pole for a color-dressed amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-182: For simplicity, let the group of circular permutations on $n$ elements be denoted $P_n:=S_{n /\amsbb{Z _{n}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: To avoid writing long products of permuted generators we denote \begin{equation}T_\omega(1,\dots,n):=T^{\dsa_{\omega(1)}}\cdots T^{\dsa_{\omega(n)}}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-184: We begin with the color-dressed amplitude of $n+1$ particles labelled $(1,1',2,\dots,n)$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{YMS:scalar}}_{n+1}= \sum_{\omega\,\in \,P_{n+1}} \Tr\Big(T_\omega(1,1',2\dots,n) \Big) A^{\mathrm{YMS:scalar}}_{n}(\omega)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: Recall that only permutations which keep the $1,1'$ particles adjacent contribute to the most singular term in the collinear limit.
elements.sent-2411-186: Keeping only these terms, we can also equivalently write the sum over circular permutations on the remaining terms as follows: \begin{equation}A_{n+1}^{YMS:scalar}=\sum_{\sigma\,\in \,S_{n-1}} \Tr\Big(T^{\dsa_1}T^{\dsa_{1'}}T_\sigma(2,\dots,n)\Big)A^{YMS:scalar}_{n+1}(1,1',\sigma) \nonumber -\Tr\Big(T^{\dsa_{1'}}T^{\dsa_{1}}T_\sigma(2,\dots,n)\Big)A^{YMS:scalar}_{n+1}(1,1',\sigma)+\dots \,,\end{equation} the negative sign arising from the Parke-Taylor factor in the partial amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-187: We can collect the two terms together to get \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}_{n+1}^{\mathrm{YMS:scalar}}=f_{11'1^\star}\!\!\!\sum_{\sigma\,\in \,S_{n-1}}\Tr\Big(T^{\dsa_{1^\star}}T_\sigma(2,\dots,n)\Big)A_{n+1}^{\mathrm{YMS:scalar}}(1,1',\sigma)+\dots\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-188: Now taking the collinear limit $s_{11'}\to 0$, which passes through the color structure, we have \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}_{n+1}^{\mathrm{YMS:scalar}}=\frac{1}{s_{11'}}f_{11'1^\star}\!\!\!\sum_{\sigma\,\in \,S_{n-1}}\Tr\Big(T^{\dsa_{1^\star}}T_\sigma(2,\dots,n)\Big)A_{n+1}^{\mathrm{YMS}}(1^\star,\sigma)+\dots=\frac{1}{s_{11'}}f_{11'1^\star}\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{YMS}}_{n}+\dots\,.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-190: In this Winter School Project we revisited the computation of collinear limits of various amplitudes using their CHY formulation.
elements.sent-2411-191: The main case we considered was the so-called ``scaffolding" residues of amplitude of scalars in the Yang-Mills-Scalar (YMS) theory.
elements.sent-2411-192: While the scaffolding residue was introduced and studied in Ref.~\cite{Arkani-Hamed:2023jry , here we computed it using the corresponding CHY formulations.}
elements.sent-2411-193: At first sight, obtaining an $n$-gluon amplitude CHY formula from that of a $2n$-scalar amplitude in YMS seems to be a daunting task.
elements.sent-2411-194: The reason is that the scalar theory is built using a very simple CHY integrand, the pfaffian of $A$, while that of gluons uses one of the most complicated, the pfaffian of $\Psi$.
elements.sent-2411-195: Not only were we able to show how one naturally emerges from the other, but we showed how one of the most mysterious features of the $\Psi$ matrix, the $C_{ii}$ elements \begin{equation}C_{ii} = -\sum_{j=1}^n \frac{\epsilon_i\cdot k_j}{u_{ij}}\,,\end{equation} arises from the scattering equations.
elements.sent-2411-196: Some next steps include understanding how even more complicated matrices in the CHY formulation can arise from simpler ones by similar scaffolding procedure.
elements.sent-2411-197: For example, the matrix $\Pi$ in the CHY formula of Einstein-Yang-Mills amplitude has a very rich structure and it would be fascinating to see it emerge from a simple matrix.
